coredata.dc:title: p-n Junction catalysis in action: Boosting norfloxacin photodegradation with ZIF-67-based Co3O4 wrapped in MoS2 with surface functionalized graphene quantum-dot
coredata.dc:creator: Adhikari, Sangeeta
Mandal, Sandip
Kim, Do-Heyoung
coredata.dc:description: The primary challenge in photocatalytic application is to address the inefficiency in exploiting the photo-induced charge carriers. The dynamic behaviors of photogenerated carriers can be controlled through an internal electric field by building a p-n heterojunction. In this respect, p-n heterojunctions constructed from zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs) with further surface functionalization are uniquely active and can increase the charge separation due to a favorable band structure. Thus, the present work demonstrates the preparation of an excellent visible-light absorbing photocatalyst based on a Co3O4 from ZIF-67 wrapped with MoS2 and functionalized with graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which is evaluated for the photodegradation of norfloxacin. Under visible light irradiation, the as-designed GQDs-functionalized MoS2@Co3O4 p-n heterojunction photocatalyst outperforms the individual MoS2 and Co3O4 semiconductors. The remarkable degradation efficiency is mostly due to the formation of a p-n heterojunction between MoS2 and the ZIF-67-based Co3O4. The photocatalytic mechanism of the as-designed system is deduced from reactive species trapping studies. This study sheds light on the design of a photocatalytic system based on ZIF-67, and on the effect of surface functionalization, thereby expanding the potential practical application of these materials in the field of environmental remediation.

ZIF-67 derived Co3O4
Graphene quantum dots
Norfloxacin degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a and b) The XRD patterns (a) and the Raman spectra (b) of the Co3O4, MoS2, 20CMS, and 20CMS-5GQD samples; (c–f) the high-resolution XPS results for the 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst, including (c) Co 2p, (d) Mo 3d, (e) O 1 s, and (f) S 2p and N 1 s.

(a–c) FE-SEM micrographs of (a) the pristine Co3O4, (b) the 20CMS, and (c) the 20CMS-5GQD; (d) the EDS spectrum and elemental mappings of the 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst; (e–g) the TEM (e) and HR-TEM (f and g) micrographs of the 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst.

(a) The photocurrent response densities, (b) the EIS Nyquist plots, (c) the PL spectra, and (d) the UV–Visible DRS analysis of the pristine Co3O4, the pristine MoS2, the 20CMS, and the 20CMS-5GQD; (e) the Tauc plots of the pristine MoS2 and Co3O4; (f) the Mott-Schottky plot of the pristine MoS2 and Co3O4 photocatalysts.

Schematic diagrams showing (a) the possible band structure of the 20CMS photocatalyst before and (b) after contact under visible light irradiation, and (c) the possible charge transfer mechanism in the 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst after contact under visible light irradiation.

The time-dependent photodegradation curves (a), kinetic curves (b), and rate constants (c) of the Co3O4, MoS2, 5CMS, 10CMS, 20CMS, and 40 CMS catalysts. The time-dependent photodegradation curves (d), kinetic curves (e), and rate constants (f) of the 20CMS-2.5GQD, 20CMS-5GQD, and 20CMS-10GQD catalysts.

(a and b) The time-dependent photodegradation and kinetic (inset) curves (a), and pH-dependent rate constants (b) of the 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst. (c and d) The time-dependent photodegradation curves of the 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst according to (c) the presence of inorganic anions and (d) the water quality. (e) The percentage total organic carbon (amount of NFX mineralization). (f) Four consecutive photocatalytic reactions using the 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst.
The time-dependent photodegradation (a), and kinetic rate constants (b) of NFX over the 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst in the absence (control) and presence of various scavengers.
Preparation of graphene quantum dots functionalized on MoS2 wrapped ZIF-67 based Co3O4 photocatalyst.
Photodegradation efficiency and kinetic data obtained for the degradation of norfloxacin over different photocatalysts.

row.entry: Parameters: Norfloxacin = 20 mg/L, catalyst loading = 20 mg, and reaction time = 90 min

head.ce:title: p-n Junction catalysis in action: Boosting norfloxacin photodegradation with ZIF-67-based Co3O4 wrapped in MoS2 with surface functionalized graphene quantum-dot

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The primary challenge in photocatalytic application is to address the inefficiency in exploiting the photo-induced charge carriers. The dynamic behaviors of photogenerated carriers can be controlled through an internal electric field by building a p-n heterojunction. In this respect, p-n heterojunctions constructed from zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs) with further surface functionalization are uniquely active and can increase the charge separation due to a favorable band structure. Thus, the present work demonstrates the preparation of an excellent visible-light absorbing photocatalyst based on a Co3O4 from ZIF-67 wrapped with MoS2 and functionalized with graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which is evaluated for the photodegradation of norfloxacin. Under visible light irradiation, the as-designed GQDs-functionalized MoS2@Co3O4 p-n heterojunction photocatalyst outperforms the individual MoS2 and Co3O4 semiconductors. The remarkable degradation efficiency is mostly due to the formation of a p-n heterojunction between MoS2 and the ZIF-67-based Co3O4. The photocatalytic mechanism of the as-designed system is deduced from reactive species trapping studies. This study sheds light on the design of a photocatalytic system based on ZIF-67, and on the effect of surface functionalization, thereby expanding the potential practical application of these materials in the field of environmental remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to rapid industrialization, problems associated with energy and the environment are increasing exponentially [1]. Hence, continued industrial development requires the further generation of fuel while controlling environmental pollution via the use of renewable and sustainable energy [2]. Among the various causes of environment pollution, antibiotics and personal care products make significant contributions [3]. For example, the fluoroquinolone antibiotic norfloxacin (NFX) is widely used in therapeutic treatment and is largely released into the environment, where it can have negative effects on the ecosystem and human health [4,5]. In this regard, light-driven photocatalysts using semiconductor technology provide an excellent choice for highly efficient environmental remediation [6–8]. Photocatalytic technology is an environmentally friendly approach, with solar energy as the driving force for reactions such as hydrogen generation, pollutant degradation, and energy conversion [9,10]. In practical application, however, the photocatalytic efficiency is limited by high levels of photogenerated charge carrier recombination, poor catalyst stability, and poor light absorption ability [11,12]. To combat these issues, strategies such as metal-ion deposition, noble/non-noble metal doping, semiconductor recombination, and heterojunction formation have been implemented [13–15]. In particular, the heterojunction photocatalytic system offers an efficient approach to overcoming the charge-carrier recombination issue and supporting the photocatalytic performance by generating a spatial electric potential difference between two or more materials [16,17]. Especially, heterojunction systems derived from metal organic frameworks (MOFs) have attracted significant interest due to their large specific surface area, structural diversity, and high porosity, which provide them with numerous potential applications in photocatalysis [18,19].

The photocatalytic degradation performance of a given system is significantly influenced by the choice of photocatalyst materials. The removal of chemical compounds from water by light-induced catalysis has traditionally been performed using transition materials such as ZnO, TiO2, etc., which function under ultraviolet light due to their high-energy bandgaps, and have limited utility under visible light [20–22]. However, the transition metal oxide Co3O4 is a semiconductor with a comparatively narrow bandgap and excellent visible light absorption properties, thus leading to its frequent use in photocatalytic degradation systems [23,24]. The Co3O4-based photocatalysts are also known to exhibit high chemical and physical stability along with environmental friendliness [25,26]. Heterojunction systems constructed from Co3O4-based MOFs can form nanostructures with large surface areas and can serve as hosts for the growth of other suitable semiconductor materials to form an interface for the facile separation of photogenerated charge carriers [27,28]. In addition, the Co3O4-based MOF combines the special physical and chemical properties of both the MOF and the Co3O4 to contribute to an enhanced photocatalytic efficiency [29]. Specifically, the MOF system contains hollow cavities in which the absorbed light can be reflected and refracted multiple times, thereby lengthening its residence time and increasing the effective production of photogenerated electrons and holes [30,31]. Nevertheless, the electron transport pathway and rate of electron transfer during the photocatalytic reaction must be improved by combining the Co3O4-based MOF with a third component to generate a unique heterojunction semiconductor combination [32,33]. In this regard, the layered transition metal sulfide, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2), has attracted significant attention due to its narrow bandgap of 1.78 eV, potent oxidizing capacity, tuneable interlayer spacing, visible light absorption, and dense active sites [34,35]. For instance, a recent study found that the metallic nature of MoS2 can promote the absorption of electrons and lower the charge transfer resistance [36]. Moreover, a further improvement in the performance has been achieved by the addition of quantum dots to the heterojunction for the effective utilization of visible light [37,38]. In previous studies by the present research group, the degradation of the antibiotic ofloxacin was shown to be more efficient when graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were deposited on the ternary system, compared to the deposition of the noble metal silver (Ag)[14,39]. As in other studies, this result demonstrated that a heterojunction with a high charge-carrier separation efficiency can be achieved by using GQDs to enhance both the light response and the photocatalytic performance[40]. Additionally, previous study has shown that the unique edge effects and quantum confinement characteristics of the graphene lattice make the GQDs more efficient photocatalysts than conventional quantum dots [41].

In the present study, a hierarchical growing technique is used to create a p-n heterojunction between a Co3O4-based MOF and MoS2 after GQD embellishment of the catalyst surface in order to capitalize on the abovementioned advantages of each component while compensating for their individual disadvantages. The interfacial charge-carrier transfer is made possible by the difference between the band edges of Co3O4 and MoS2. In general, the production of hierarchical structures from MOF-based systems results in greater specific surface areas and optimized performances, while the controlled self-assembly of nanomaterials is crucial for effective photocatalysis. In comparison to the single photocatalysts, the Co3O4@MoS2@GQD system delivers the maximum photocatalytic removal of NFX by fully utilizing the widely dispersed active sites and active substances. Moreover, the participation of GQDs and the as-fabricated catalyst increase the range of choices for electron oxidation reactions. The effects of the ternary material in enhancing the photocatalytic reactions are systematically described and evaluated, and is shown to result from the advanced surface reactive sites generated by the high specific surface area, along with the increased charge generation, transfer, and recombination capability.

All the chemicals used for the synthesis and experiments were purchased from Sigma Aldrich and were used without any purification. Initially, ZIF-67 (Co(1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium bis(trifluormethylsulfonyl)imide)2 or Co(hmim)2) was prepared by separately dissolving cobalt nitrite (Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.873 g) in methanol (40 ml) to obtain Solution 1, and 2-methylimidazole (0.984 g) in methanol (10 ml) to obtain Solution 2, with magnetic stirring. Solution 1 was then slowly added to Solution 2 by using a 50-ml burette with continuous stirring until the color changed to purple, after which the solution was aged for 24 h at room temperature. The purple precipitate was then centrifugally separated by washing with ethanol, and dried in a hot-air oven at 60 °C for 12 h. To convert the ZIF-67 to the Co3O4 form, the obtained ZIF-67 powder was uniformly spread over an alumina crucible boat and treated at 300 °C for 3 h in a tube furnace to produce a black-colored Co3O4-based powder.

To prepare the Co3O4@MoS2 composite catalyst, a facile hydrothermal reaction was performed. In this procedure, Co3O4 (100 mg) was uniformly dispersed in deionized water via ultrasonication for 30 min. To this dispersion, certain amounts of ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate ((NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O) and thiourea (CH4N2S) were added and magnetically stirred for complete dissolution. The homogeneously dispersed solution was then hydrothermally treated at 180 °C for 15 h. After the reaction, the precipitates were centrifugally collected while washing several times with ethanol and deionized water, and then dried as above. During the preparation, weight ratios of 5, 10, 20, and 40 % MoS2 relative to Co3O4 were used to generate the samples designated as 5CMS, 10CMS, 20CMS, and 40CMS, respectively, where CMS = Co3O4@MoS2. In addition, pure MoS2 was synthesized following the above-mentioned procedure, but without Co3O4. Finally, a 100-mg dispersion of the 20CMS sample was added to various volumes (2.5, 5, and 10 ml) of a GQD suspension to generate the Co3O4@MoS2@GQD catalysts designated hereafter as the 20CMS-2.5GQD, the 20CMS-5GQD, and the 20CMS-10GQD, respectively. The 20CMS was selected for this work because the results in Section 3.3 demonstrate this to be the optimal formulation. The catalysts were isolated by the process described above. The GQDs were prepared according to a previously-published procedure. [14]. In brief, citric acid (2 g) was added to a beaker and pyrolyzed directly at 200 °C using a heating mantle. After 20 min, the citric acid was seen to transform into an orange liquid, to which 100 ml of 1 M NaOH was added dropwise with vigorous magnetic stirring. The resulting GQD suspension was kept at a neutral pH and chilled to 4 °C before being used. A complete preparation process is illustrated schematically in Scheme 1 below.

The performance of the as-fabricated catalyst was evaluated according to the photodegradation of norfloxacin (NFX) under simulated light using a 150 W Xe-lamp (HS Technologies). First, a 20 mg/L solution of NFX was magnetically agitated for 30 min in the dark to attain adsorption–desorption equilibrium. The solution was then subjected to visible-light irradiation, and 1.5-ml aliquots of the reaction solution were removed every 15 min for a total of 90 min. The catalyst was centrifugally separated from each aliquot and subjected to absorbance measurement at the NFX absorption wavelength (λ = 276 nm) by using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Varian Cary, Agilent). The absorbance of NFX was then calculated using the formula:
photodegradation rate = (C0 – Ct)/C0 x 100 %.
where, C0 is the concentration after adsorption–desorption equilibrium and Ct is the final concentration after photocatalytic reaction.

The crystal structures of the as-prepared catalysts were examined via X-ray diffraction (XRD; Rigaku X-ray diffractometer). The morphological and microstructural features were examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) using an FEI TECNAI G2 F20 system, and by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) using a JEOL JSM-7500F system. The light absorption characteristics at various wavelengths were examined via ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS; SHIMADZU UV-2600). In addition, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained on a JASCO FP-8550 spectrofluorometer, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Thermo Scientific ESCALAB -MKII) was used to determine the oxidation states and chemical properties of the material. The Fourier transform Raman spectra of the as-prepared photocatalysts were acquired on a Bruker FRA 106/S spectrometer with an Nd-YAG laser radiation excitation source.

The photoelectrochemical measurements were performed using a three-electrode setup with the material as the working electrode, a platinum plate as the counter electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, and an aqueous solution of Na2SO4 (0.5 mol/L) as the electrolyte. To fabricate the working electrode, 5 mg of the material was dissolved in 500 µL of ethanol and a slurry was generated by ultrasonication for 30 min. After this, 20 µL of the uniformly-dispersed slurry was spin coated onto a 1 cm2 area of a 1 cm × 1.5 cm fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass substrate at 1500 rpm for 30 s, and allowed to dry overnight at 60 °C. A 150 W Xenon lamp was used as a source of light illumination for the photoelectrochemical measurements, all of which were recorded at ambient temperature. In addition, the ability of the as-synthesized materials to transmit charge was evaluated via electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS; Solartron frequency response analyzer).

The XRD patterns of the Co3O4, MoS2, 20CMS, and 20CMS-5GQD are presented in Fig. 1
a, while that of the ZIF-67 precursor is presented in Figure S1 of the Supporting Information. Thus, the ZIF-67 derived Co3O4 (pink line, Fig. 1 a) exhibits distinct diffraction peaks at 19.16°, 31.36°, 36.99°, 44.93°, 59.54°, and 65.33° due to the respective (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), and (440) crystal facets of cubic Co3O4 (JCPDS No. 43–1003). Moreover, the high phase purity of the material is confirmed by the absence of any additional peaks from other phases of cobalt oxide. These results are similar to those obtained for Co3O4 derived from ZIF-67 in a previous Co3O4/ZnO heterojunction study [42]. Meanwhile, the XRD pattern of the pristine MoS2 (wine colored line, Fig. 1 a) exhibits major peaks at 2θ = 16.74°, 32.61°, and 58.25° corresponding to the (002), (100), and (110) crystal planes (JCPDS No.37–1492). Some weak peaks are also observed, which are indicative of poor crystallinity. By comparison, peaks for both MoS2 and Co3O4 can be clearly observed in the XRD pattern of the 20CMS (green line, Fig. 1 a), including the MoS2 peak at 32.61°, although the higher-angle XRD peak of MoS2 at 58.25° overlaps with the Co3O4 peak at 59.54°. Here, no additional MoS2 peaks are observed, thereby indicating that both component materials maintain their integrity during preparation, despite the temperature used for hydrothermal growth. This may be due to the low precursor concentration of MoS2. It should be noted that similar diffraction patterns have been observed for MoS2-containing composites in previous studies [43], and that the slight peak broadening observed in the as-fabricated 20CMS heterojunction photocatalyst after composite formation is consistent with that observed in previously-reported composites of UiO-66-NH2 embedded with MoS2 [44]. Finally, the XRD pattern of the 20CMS-5GQD (blue line Fig. 1 a) does not show any significant difference compared to that of the 20CMS. Although the literature suggests that the characteristic diffraction peak of GQDs should be observed at 2θ = 26°, it is not observed in the present case, which may be due to the low quantity and crystallinity of GQDs [45]. These results confirm that the growth of MoS2 on the ZIF-67 based Co3O4, followed by the deposition of GQDs, yields a composite material in which all components exhibit high phase purity.

The bonding behaviors of the Co3O4 and MoS2 in the 20CMS and 20CMS-5GQD are revealed by the Raman spectra in Fig. 1 b. Here, the pristine Co3O4 exhibits sharp Raman bands at 475, 520, and 681 cm−1. Of these, the first two can be attributed to the Eg and F2g vibrational modes of the tetrahedral sites and motions of the octahedral oxygen, whereas the third band is due to the A1g vibrational mode of the octahedral site [46]. Meanwhile, the pristine MoS2 exhibits sharp Raman bands at 290, 382, 412, and 452 cm−1 indicative of the hexagonal phase [47,48]. Among these, the bands at 382 and 412 cm−1 are due to the respective in-plane E1 2g and out-of-plane A1g vibrational modes, in agreement with the literature [47], while the small bands at 290 and 452 cm−1 correspond to the E1g and longitudinal acoustic phonon modes, respectively. In the 20CMS photocatalyst, the Raman signals from MoS2 are more prominent than those of the Co3O4, which is similar to previous observations in a MoS2/g-C3N4 system [49]. Finally, with the addition of GQDs, two new Raman signals are observed in the higher wavenumber region for the 20CMS-5GQD. In previous studies, the Raman signals for GQD were observed between 1000 and 2000 cm−1, with those at 1325 and 1580 cm−1 being the most intense. In the present study, the broad peaks centered at 1367 and 1575 cm−1 are observed due to the G and D bands of the C = C bonds. This is due, in turn, to defects in the zigzag armchair structure, as reported in the literature [50,51].

The surface composition and chemical states of the various components in the 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst are revealed by the XPS survey scans in Figure S2 , while the corresponding high-resolution Co 2p, Mo 3d, O 1 s, S 2p, and N 1 s spectra are presented in Fig. 1 c–-f. Thus, Fig. 1 c displays the typical peaks of the Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 spin orbit doublets, with a spin energy difference of about 15.8 eV. Here, the binding energies of 779.55 and 795.37 eV correspond to the Co3+ valence state, while those of 780.92 and 797.90 eV correspond to the Co2+ valence state [52,53]. In addition, shake-up satellite peaks are observed at 783.94 and 787.99 eV for the Co 2p3/2 state, and at 801.98 and 804.88 eV for the Co 2p1/2 state [54]. The detailed peak assignments, including the peak area and other fitting parameters, for the Co 2p spectrum are provided in Table S1 . Meanwhile, the high-resolution Mo 3d spectrum in Fig. 1 d exhibits peaks at binding energies of 229.10 and 232.08 eV due to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2, respectively [55], thereby indicating the presence of the Mo4+ state in the as-grown MoS2, along with peaks at 232.51 and 235.38 eV due to the Mo6+ state from the partial oxidation [56]. Interestingly, the peak fitting results exhibit lower-intensity peaks at 230.51 and 233.65 eV, which correspond to the molybdenum oxysulfide, as reported in the literature [57,58]. The detailed peak assignments, including the peak area and other fitting parameters, for the Mo 3d spectrum are provided in Table S2
.

The C 1 s spectrum in the bottom panel of Fig. 1 e is deconvolved into three peaks centered at binding energies 284.80, 285.92, and 288.39 eV due to the various GQD features, including: (i) the characteristic sp2 hybridization between the C = C and C–C bonds, (ii) the sp3 hybridization with functional groups such as C–O, C–S, and C-N, and (iii) the C = O groups, respectively [45]. Meanwhile, the O 1 s spectrum (top panel, Fig. 1 e) is deconvolved into four peaks labelled O1, O2, O3, and O4 at binding energies of 528.55, 530.27, 531.89, and 535.26 eV, respectively. These correspond respectively to the surface lattice O2– ions of the metal oxides, the oxygen vacancies, the adsorbed oxygen, and the hydroxyl groups present in the photocatalyst [42]. The presence of MoS2 is confirmed by the S 2 s peak at a binding energy of 225.81 eV in the bottom panel of Fig. 1 f [59], which is deconvoluted into S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 peaks at binding energies of 162.02 eV and 163.23 eV due to the S2– chemical state [59]. Meanwhile, the N 1 s XPS result (top panel, Fig. 1 f) is deconvolved into three peaks with binding energies of 398.46, 400.67, and 402.19 eV, corresponding to the respective C–N = C, N–(C)3, and C–N–H groups of GQDs [60]. Taken together, these XPS results indicate the presence of all the expected species and elements in the composite catalyst, and are well supported by the existing literature reports [43,61]. In addition, the characteristic behavior of composites formed with MoS2 is fully explained in a beautiful review by Swain et al. [62].

The surface morphologies of the Co3O4, the 20CMS, and the 20CMS-5GQD samples are revealed by the FE-SEM and TEM images in Fig. 2 . FE-SEM micrograph of the as prepared ZIF-67 polyhedrons can be found in Figure S1 . Here, the FE-SEM micrograph of the pristine Co3O4 (Fig. 2 a) reveals polyhedral particles with well-defined facets, distinct edges, and diameters of approximately 500 nm. By contrast, the Co3O4 facets in the 20CMS photocatalyst are seen to be rough with some flake structures due to the growth of MoS2, in agreement to the abovementioned XRD results. Meanwhile, the FE-SEM image of the 20CMS photocatalyst in Fig. 2 c reveals no significant morphological change due to the addition of GQDs, although the structures appear quite agglomerated. This agglomeration can influence the optical absorption properties of the as-prepared photocatalyst by scattering the incident radiation and blocking the photoactive sites, which can affect the overall photocatalytic efficiency and stability. To avoid such catalyst agglomeration, the photocatalytic experiments are conducted under constant magnetic stirring. Furthermore, the EDS analysis of the 20CMS-5QD photocatalyst in Fig. 2 d reveals the presence of the elements C, O, S, Mo and Co, while the corresponding elemental mappings of a single 20CMS-5GQD polyhedron indicate that the elements are well distributed, with Co3O4 in the core, MoS2 on the surface, and carbon all over the photocatalyst surface. Similar microstructural features are observed in the TEM micrograph of the 20CMS-5QD photocatalyst in Fig. 2 e, where the particle appears to be partially hollow, while the HR-TEM micrograph in Fig. 2 f clearly reveals the growth of MoS2 along with the GQD particles (small circles). The partially hollow structures benefit not only the light absorption property via multiple refractions, but also the surface adsorption of contaminant molecules by the catalyst due to the high surface area provided by the hollow cavities. Further, the lattice fringes in Fig. 2 g exhibit spacing’s of 0.244 and 0.61 nm, which can be identified with the respective (311) and (002) crystal planes of cubic Co3O4 and hexagonal MoS2, in agreement with the abovementioned X-ray diffraction patterns, thereby confirming the structure formation. In this image, the deposited GQDs on the MoS2 flakes are more clearly observed as dark spots (indicated by the circles). Taken together, the above analyses of the phases, bonding, chemical states, and morphological structures confirm the formation of the pristine precursors and the composite photocatalysts with high purity.

The separation effectiveness and migration rate of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs in the as-prepared catalysts under light illumination are directly reflected by the transient photocurrent density analysis in Fig. 3
a. Here, the 20CMS-5GQD clearly generates a higher photocurrent than either the pristine Co3O4 or the pristine MoS2, thereby demonstrating that these two components have an enhanced ability to generate photocurrents when in direct contact with each another. Moreover, the photocurrent generated by the 20CMS-5GQD is higher than that generated by the 20CMS photocatalyst, thereby demonstrating the added effect of the GQDs. This increased photocurrent intensity results in both a higher separation efficiency and the generation of more electrons. Moreover, the photocurrent responsiveness of the 20CMS-5GQD exhibits a lower tendency to degrade over time, which may be related to the fact that the photo-corrosion of this material is stifled by the transfer and consumption of holes [63].

The ability of the as-synthesized materials to transmit charge is revealed by the EIS Nyquist plots in Fig. 3 b, where the resistance to charge transfer is indicated by the diameter of the semi-circular arc at the front end [64]. Thus, the pristine Co3O4 exhibits the largest arc diameter and, hence, the highest resistance of all the as-prepared materials, thereby completely stifling the transfer of electrons. By contrast, the pristine MoS2 exhibits a significantly lower arc radius and, hence, a lower resistance. Meanwhile, due to the combination of the more conductive MoS2 with the less conductive Co3O4, the amount of charge transfer inhibition provided by the 20CMS is slightly lower, and its conductivity slightly higher, than that of the pristine MoS2. Similarly, the additional surface sensitization by the GQDs further affects the charge transfer resistance, but not to a great extent. However, a clearer picture may be provided by the steady-state photoluminescence PL spectra in Fig. 3 c. Here, the 20CMS exhibits a noticeably lower PL intensity than the pristine MoS2, and the pristine Co3O4, thereby indicating that the modification of Co3O4 with MoS2 effectively suppresses the charge transfer behavior and lengthens the decay lifespan of electron-hole pairs. Moreover, the 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst exhibits a sizable capacity for visible photocatalysis.

One of the major properties of the photocatalysts is their ability to harvest light energy. However, the light harvesting ability is dependent on the band structure of the material. This structure is determined from the UV–vis DRS and Mott-Schottky (MS) analyses in Fig. 3 d, e and f. Thus, in Fig. 3 d, the Co3O4 exhibits a strong absorption in the wavelength range of 500–800 nm, while the pristine MoS2 exhibits sharp absorptions at 575 and 725 nm. Meanwhile, the light absorption property of the 20CMS photocatalyst is seen to increase relative to the pristine materials, which is reflected in the bandgap value, as discussed later. Moreover, a further improvement in the light absorption behavior is observed in the 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst due to the presence of the GQDs, with multiple absorption peaks ranging from 300 to 700 nm. From the Tauc plots in Fig. 3 e, the energy bandgaps of the Co3O4, and MoS2 are 1.89, and 1.72 eV, respectively, in agreement with the previous literature [14,61]. These results are also consistent with the above PL analysis, and demonstrate that the combination of the two materials to create a heterojunction structure, followed by surface sensitization, makes it possible to increase the visible light absorption, decrease the bandgap energy, and increase the susceptibility of the catalytic material to visible light excitation.

Furthermore, the energy band locations of the pristine Co3O4 and MoS2 samples can be obtained from the MS curves in Fig. 3 f. In general, the MS curve can be used to determine whether the material is a p-type or n-type semiconductor, and to identify the potential at which there is no depletion layer at the junction between the semiconductor and the electrolyte (termed the flat band potential, Efb). The curves in Fig. 3 f indicate that the Co3O4 and MoS2 are respectively p-type and n-type semiconductors, with Efb values of 1.02 V and –0.97 V. In general, an n-type semiconductor has a conduction band (CB) potential that is 0.1–0.3 V lower than the Efb, while a p-type semiconductor has a valence band (VB) potential that is 0.1–0.3 V higher than the Efb. Hence, an applied potential of 0.2 V is first used to identify the CB and VB values of the n-type MoS2 and the p-type Co3O4 as –1.17 and + 1.22 V, respectively, relative to the saturated calomel electrode (SCE). Then, the SCE values are converted to the standard hydrogen electrode (SHE) values by using the formula ENHE = ESCE + 0.241, giving a CB potential of –0.929 eV for the MoS2 and a VB potential of 1.461 eV for the Co3O4. Finally, by combining the previously-determined energy bandgaps of 1.89 and 1.72 eV for the Co3O4 and MoS2, respectively, the VB potential of the MoS2 and the CB potential of Co3O4 can be predicted to be 0.791 and –0.429 eV, respectively. This result is summarized by the energy band diagram in Fig. 4
a.

In general, the major charge carriers in n-type semiconductors are electrons (e.g., photoelectrons), giving them Efb values that are close to the CB, whereas p-type semiconductors have holes as the major charge carriers, and Efb values that are close to the VB. In the p-n heterostructure system, an internal electric field develops at the interface between the p- and n-type semiconductors due to charge carrier diffusion between the two semiconductors. Thus, the internal electric field in the p-n heterojunction system effectively divides the electrons from the holes. Upon light irradiation, the photoinduced electrons move quickly from the CB of the Co3O4 to the CB of the MoS2, where they interact with O2 to form O2 –. At the same time, the holes migrate from the VB of the MoS2 to the VB of the Co3O4. Based on these bandgaps and the electron transport direction, the Co3O4-MoS2 p-n heterojunction can be classified as a type-II band alignment heterojunction (Fig. 4 b). Moreover, the charge carrier separation and transfer properties are further improved by the introduction of the GQDs (Fig. 4 c), as expected due to the reputed excellent electron transport properties of GQDs [65].

The photocatalytic performances of the individual precursors and the as-prepared CMS catalysts are demonstrated by the photodegradation of NFX in Fig. 5
a. Here, the concentration of NFX in solution remains almost constant (C/C0 ∼ 1) over time in the absence of a photocatalyst, thereby indicating that NFX is very stable in the presence of direct visible light. Meanwhile, the pristine MoS2 results in a slight improvement, with the degradation of some NFX (21 %) during 90 min of illumination. By comparison, the pristine Co3O4 exhibits a greater photocatalytic performance, with the removal of 38 % NFX in 90 min. Further improvements are observed in the presence of the various Co3O4@MoS2 composite catalysts, with performances of 74, 77, 98, and 62 % for the 5CMS, 10CMS, 20CMS, and 40CMS, respectively, during 90 min of irradiation. Thus, the 20CMS provides the best catalytic performance towards NFX degradation among the various CMS photocatalysts. The enhanced degradation efficiency of the CMS photocatalysts relative to the individual precursors can be attributed to the formation of a heterojunction between the Co3O4 and MoS2, which helps to separate the photogenerated electron-hole pairs and provide easy charge transfer during the photodegradation process. Meanwhile, the decrease in the performance of the 40CMS relative to the others is explained by an increase in particle agglomeration during light exposure as the loading of MoS2 over Co3O4 is increased, thereby decreasing the amount of light exposure on the active surface. The corresponding kinetic curves are presented in Fig. 5 b, and are seen to be well-fitted to the pseudo-first order kinetic equation until the 60th minute. Hence, the kinetic rate constants (min−1) for the 60th min are plotted in Fig. 5 c. Here, the rate constant for the 20CMS is 2.2 × 10–2 min−1, which is 5.5 and 14.6 times higher than those of the pristine Co3O4 (0.43 × 10–2 min−1) and the pristine MoS2 (0.15 × 10–2 min−1), respectively, due to the effective charge separation via the abovementioned heterojunction effect. Furthermore, the rate constant of the 20CMS is 2.75, 2.00, and 3.14 times higher than those of the 5CMS, 10CMS, and 40CMS catalysts, respectively, thereby confirming that the 20CMS is the optimal formulation.

The effects of various GQD loadings on the NFX degradation performance of the optimal 20CMS catalyst are presented in Fig. 5 d. Here, while there is an almost negligible adsorption of NFX in the dark after the addition of GQDs deposition, the photocatalytic performance is seen to have increased, with the 20CMS-5GQD catalyst giving the highest degradation rate of ∼ 95 % in 60 min. Further, the corresponding photodegradation kinetics are estimated according to the linear fitting in Fig. 5 e, and are seen to match exactly the pseudo first-order kinetic equation. The corresponding photodegradation rate constants are shown in Fig. 5 f. Here, the 20CMS-5GQD exhibits a rate constant of 3.8 × 10–2 min−1, which is 1.52, 1.35, and 1.72 times greater than that of the 20CMS-2.5GQD (2.5 × 10–2 min−1), the 20CMS-10GQD (2.8 × 10–2 min−1), and the 20CMS (2.2 × 10–2 min−1), respectively. A tabular column is presented that displays all the photodegradation efficiency and rate constants data for easy comparison (Table 1 ).

The aquatic environment of any contaminant has great significance for the rate and percentage of photodegradation. In the present study, NFX primarily exists as positive species at pH < 6.1, whereas its antibiotic forms are neutral and amphoteric at pH values ranging from 6.1 to 8.7. At a pH of > 8.7, the negatively-charged species are predominant [66]. Thus, a study of the pH-dependent photodegradation is crucial to an understanding of the antibiotic’s degradation efficiency in a more complex aquatic system. To this end, the zero point charge (zpc) of the optimal 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst was determined, and its pH at zpc (pHzpc) was found to be 6.2, as shown in Figure S3 . This indicates that the surface of the as-prepared catalyst will be positively charged below a pH of 6.2, and negatively charged at a pH above 6.2. Therefore, under low pH conditions (pH = 3), the surfaces of the 20CMS-5GQD and NFX both exist in positive form, thus leading to mutual repulsion. This is reflected in a reduced photocatalytic activity (Fig. 6
a). Interestingly, the photocatalytic performance at pH = 9 is initially greater and becomes similar to that at pH = 7 over the course of time. This might be attributed to the hydrogen boding effect between the 20CMS-5GQD and NFX, which is known to occur under neutral conditions, and may also occur at pH = 9 due to a slow transition of surface charge between the NFX and 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst. At pH > 9, the surfaces of the photocatalyst and NFX are both negatively charged, so that mutual electrostatic repulsion occurs again, and a corresponding reduction in the photodegradation performance is again observed. For data consistency and comparison, the effects of pH on the photodegradation kinetics until the 60th min are shown in the inset of Fig. 6 a. Here, the experimentally obtained data points are seen to be well fitted to the pseudo first-order equation, and the corresponding rate constants are plotted in Fig. 6 b. Thus, the kinetic rate constant of the 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst is found to be 1.9 × 10–2, 3.8 × 10–2, 5.4 × 10–2, and 2.2 × 10–2 min−1 at pH values of 3, 7, 9, and 11, respectively.

High concentrations of inorganic anions in complex wastewater systems may cause the photocatalyst to become inactive and shorten its lifetime during the photocatalytic degradation of actual environmental contaminants, which is a crucial factor affecting the viability of photocatalysis. To study this effect on the 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst, 1-mM concentrations of four inorganic species (NO3 –, Cl–, CO3 2–, and SO4 2–) were added to the standard NFX solution, and the experimental results are presented in Fig. 6 c. Here, the photodegradation of NFX is seen to be negligibly affected by the presence of Cl– and SO4 2–, but significantly decreases in the presence of NO3 – and CO3 2–. In the the latter case, the CO3 2– anion has a particularly significant reaction quenching effect due to its fast reaction with the photo-generated hydroxyl radical [67,68].

In addition, the photodegradation of NFX is affected by the water quality. To test this effect, experiments were conducted using laboratory-standard deionized water and sampled lake water (Gwangjuho lake, Gwangju) to prepare the NFX aqueous solutions. The results in Fig. 6 d indicate a slight retardation in photodegradation of the NFX solution that was prepared using the lake water. This may be related to the modest alkalinity of the lake water (pH = 7.82) and the presence of organic matter and other contaminant species.

The effectiveness of any remediation approach is revealed by mineralization, which is related to the potential decrease in the mass of a given pollutant. Hence, the potential toxicity of an organic pollutant, and the potential of a given remediation technology, can be inferred experimentally from this mass loss. Large quantities of organic pollutants are converted during mineralization into compounds that are easily degradable [67]. A high catalyst dose and the use of peroxides are reported to be beneficial for mineralization [67,69]. In the case of the optimal 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst, the rate of mineralization during 90 min of visible-light irradiation is indicated by the percentage total organic carbon content in Fig. 6 e. Here, the mineralization rate is initially low, and increases with time to reach about 59 % at 90 min. This suggests that the mineralization of NFX is initiated by the generated intermediate products during the photocatalytic reactions.

The practical use of the as-prepared catalyst is critically dependent on its physico-chemical stability and reusability, as these properties are related to the ultimate cost of real-time application. To demonstrate the stability of the optimal 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst, four reaction cycles are shown in Fig. 6 f. At the end of each cycle, the catalyst was collected and cleaned via micro-centrifugation, separation, washing, and drying prior to use in the next cycle. The results reveal a slight reduction in the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst from almost 100 % in the first cycle to 95 % in the fourth. This can be correlated with some loss of catalyst loss during each cycle and to partial catalyst deactivation via the blockage of active reaction sites. Furthermore, the XRD and XPS measurements of the 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst obtained before use and after the 4th cycle are compared in Fig. S4 a and b, revealing almost no significant difference. In addition, the FE-SEM micrograph of the 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst after the 4th cycle (Fig. S4c) shows reasonably distinct polyhedral particles without significant morphological alteration compared to those of the unused catalyst in Fig. 2 c. These findings demonstrate that the as-prepared 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst exhibits superior photocatalytic capabilities.

The results of active-species trapping experiments during the visible-light photocatalytic degradation of NFX over the optimal 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst in the presence of various scavengers are presented in Fig. 7
a. Here, 5 mM concentrations of ammonium oxalate (AO), silver nitrate (SN), and benzoquinone (BZQ) are used as scavengers of holes (h+), electrons (e–), and ·O2 – radicals, respectively. Thus, when AO is added to the NFX solution, the photocatalytic performance of the 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst decreases moderately in comparison to the control, thereby indicating that h+ are indeed participating in the degradation, but are not the major active species in the visible-light driven photodegradation of NFX. However, when either SN or BZQ is added to the NFX solution, the visible-light photocatalytic efficacy of the 20CMS-5GQD photocatalyst is dramatically diminished, thus indicating that electrons and the ·O2 – radicals are the predominant active species in NFX photodegradation. These results are further supported by the kinetic rate constants obtained from the pseudo first order-fitting of the experimental data as shown in Fig. 7 b, which indicates 6.7-, 15.1-, and 36.9-fold decreases in the rate of the NFX photodegradation in the presence of AO, SN, and BZQ, respectively, compared to the control solution.

The above results lead to the hypothesis that the photocatalytic process of the Co3O4/MoS2 p-n heterojunction works as follows: when exposed to visible light, the photosensitive Co3O4 is readily activated, thus producing electrons and holes. As shown schematically in Fig. 7 c, when an internal electric field is present, the excited electrons move from the CB of Co3O4 to the CB of MoS2, while the holes stay in the VB of the Co3O4. Thus, the p-n junction generated in the heterostructured Co3O4/MoS2 interface effectively separates the photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thereby greatly increasing the photocatalytic activity. In other words, it is possible to limit the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes to a certain extent. The internal electric field in the p-n heterojunction also increases the migration rates of the photogenerated electrons and holes. Hence, efficient charge separation can be used to increase the lifetime of the charge carriers and give them ample time to react with the reactants that are adsorbed on the surface of the photocatalyst. The improved visible-light photocatalytic activity is clearly caused by the synergetic effects of the p-n junction formed between the Co3O4 from ZIF-67 and the MoS2, as shown by the experimental and supporting data. The visible-light photocatalytic degradation performance of the 20CMS-5GQD system is significantly improved by the presence of GQDs for the following reasons: (i) the upconversion properties of the GQDs can significantly improve the light-harvesting ability of the 20CMS photocatalyst, thereby increasing the efficiency of the photocatalytic reaction, (ii) the GQDs can act as electron traps, thereby facilitating the transfer of electrons from the CB of MoS2 to the GQDs via the interface, which favors charge-carrier separation, and (iii) under the combined action of all reactive species (O2 –, e–, and h+), NFX can be degraded with more than 50 % mineralization.

Herein, a highly efficient visible-light-driven graphene quantum dot (GQD)-sensitized Co3O4@MoS2 p-n heterojunction photocatalyst was successfully synthesized by a simple precipitation-calcination-hydrothermal method. With the modification of MoS2 on the surface of the Co3O4, the composite structure showed a much lower photoluminescence intensity, and a higher photodegradation efficiency toward organic pollutants. Additionally, the GQDs were strongly able to enhance the light harvesting capacity and facilitate the charge separation. Strong coupling at their interfaces synergistically enhanced the photophysical properties. When compared to the individual Co3O4 and MoS2 components, the composite material exhibited a much greater photodegradation of norfloxacin (NFX) due to the tight coupling between the elements in the photocatalyst system, which led to enhanced photophysical characteristics. In addition, trapping investigations revealed that the primary active species during the photodegradation of NFX were ·O2 –, e–, and h+. Thus, the as-fabricated p-n junction and GQD-sensitized photocatalyst has outstanding application potential in the treatment of contaminated water.
